data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "Anthropogenic deforestation and soil erosion in palace core zones crossed critical thresholds simultaneously, collapsing energetic foundations of monumental redistributive systems", "mechanism": "Mycenaean and Hittite palace economies required massive timber inputs for bronze production (charcoal), construction, shipbuilding, and elite consumption; centuries of palace-driven extraction caused slope degradation and alluviation in Boeotian-Argolid and central Anatolian highlands; by 1200 BC transport costs for replacement timber exceeded surplus extraction capacity; agricultural yields fell on eroded slopes precisely when timber famine hit metallurgical and military sectors; palace redistribution required growing surplus to fund growing extraction costs—positive feedback to collapse; Egypt survived because Nile alluvium regenerated annually and Delta mangrove/Acacia sources were not palace-managed exhaustion zones", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia"], "excludes": ["Ugarit", "Levantine city-states", "Egyptian political survival (only ecological survival)"] }, "forbidden": [{"case": "Palynological evidence shows expanded not contracted forest cover in Mycenaean/Hittite core zones 1400-1200 BC", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Destruction layers show no reduction in architectural timber quality or bronze output pre-collapse", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egyptian Delta experienced comparable deforestation and relied on Levantine timber imports that continued uninterrupted", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Deeply atypical: foregrounds energetic and ecological economics rather than climate events or invasion. Excludes maritime Levant deliberately—Ugarit collapse would need supplementary mechanism."}
